1: Psalm 62 is attributed to Jeduthun — one of David's chief musicians (1 Chronicles 16:41-42; Psalms 25:1-6). אַךְ אֶל-אֱלֹהִים דּוּמִיָּה נַפְשִׁי (ʾak ʾel-ʾĕlōhîm dûmiyyāh napshî, "Truly my soul waits in silence for God alone") — ʾak: truly, surely (a strong affirmation-particle); dûmiyyāh: silence, stillness (from dāmam: to be still, to be silent — not the silence of despair or death but of deliberate, attentive waiting; the soldier "at ease" who has stopped striving). Napshî: my soul (the whole animated self) — the entire person oriented toward God alone. מִמֶּנּוּ יְשׁוּעָתִי (mimmennû yĕshûʿātî, "from him comes my salvation") — the salvation (yĕshûʿāh) originates exclusively from God: the preposition min (from) specifies the single source. The psalm's entire theology: God alone as the sole ground of trust.
2: אַךְ-הוּא צוּרִי וִישׁוּעָתִי מִשְׂגַּבִּי (ʾak-hûʾ ṣûrî wîshûʿātî miśgabbî, "He alone is my rock and my salvation; he is my fortress") — the triple confession: ṣûr (rock — the immovable foundation), yĕshûʿāh (salvation — the rescue), miśgāb (fortress — the elevated stronghold of Psalms 46:7). לֹא-אֶמּוֹט רַבָּה (lōʾ-ʾemmôṭ rabbāh, "I will not be greatly shaken") — māṭ: to totter, to slip; rabbāh: greatly, much. The qualifier "greatly" (rabbāh) is the first refrain's humility: the Psalmist does not claim immunity from all shaking, only that the shaking will not be total. Compare v6 where rabbāh is absent — a stronger confidence reached by the psalm's end.
3: עַד-אָנָה תְּהוֹתְתוּ עַל-אִישׁ (ʿad-ʾānāh tĕhôtĕtû ʿal-ʾîsh, "How long will all of you assault a man?") — ʿad-ʾānāh: "until when?" — the protest against prolonged oppression. Hātat (polel/hithpolel: to attack, to rush upon): the violent pressing assault. תְּרָצְּחוּ כֻּלְּכֶם (tĕrāṣĕḥû kullĕkem, "you might as well be killing him") — rāṣaḥ: to murder; the hyperbole of sustained persecution: the relentless assault amounts to murder. כְּקִיר נָטוּי כְּגָדֵר הַדְּחוּיָה (kĕqîr nāṭûy kĕgādêr haddĕḥûyāh, "you are like a leaning wall, like a tottering fence") — the enemies are the unstable structure that is about to collapse: they attack someone already weakened, and their own violence tilts them toward ruin. A leaning wall and a pushed-over fence are both about to fall — the enemies press against the righteous and lean themselves into collapse.
4: אַךְ מִשְּׂאֵתוֹ יָעֲצוּ לְהַדִּיחַ (ʾak misśĕʾētô yāʿăṣû lĕhaddîaḥ, "They only plan to bring him down from his position") — śĕʾēt: dignity, eminence, elevation (the exalted position that the enemies wish to topple). Nādaḥ (hiphil: to push away, to cast down from a height): the plan is demotion from his elevated standing. יִרְצוּ כָזָב (yirṣû kāzāb, "they delight in falsehood") — rāṣāh: to take pleasure in, to delight in (the same word as divine pleasure in Psalms 44:3 — now the enemies take pleasure in the opposite of divine truth). בְּפִיו יְבָרֲכוּ וּבְקִרְבָּם יְקַלְלוּ סֶלָה (bĕpîw yĕbārăkû ûbĕqirbām yĕqallĕlû selāh, "with their mouths they bless, but inwardly they curse") — the double speech: the outer blessing (bārak) concealing the inner curse (qālal). Selāh marks the pause on this hypocrisy.
5: אַךְ לֵאלֹהִים דּוֹמִּי נַפְשִׁי (ʾak lêʾlōhîm dômmî napshî, "Be still before God alone, my soul!") — the Psalmist turns to address himself: the imperative dômmî (be still! from dāmam) directed inward. The self-counsel of the Psalter: the Psalmist commands his own soul to the same silence he announced in v1 — now as a directive after the disruption of vv3-4. כִּי-מִמֶּנּוּ תִּקְוָתִי (kî-mimmennû tiqwātî, "for my hope comes from him") — tiqwāh: hope, expectation (from qāwāh: to wait with expectation — a cord, a thread stretched taut between the present and the promised future). In v1 it was yĕshûʿāh (salvation) from God; here it is tiqwāh (hope) from God — the inner disposition corresponding to the external salvation.
6: אַךְ-הוּא צוּרִי וִישׁוּעָתִי מִשְׂגַּבִּי — the refrain returned, almost identical to v2. The key difference: לֹא אֶמּוֹט (lōʾ ʾemmôṭ, "I will not be shaken") — without the qualifier rabbāh ("greatly") of v2. The confidence has deepened through the psalm: in v2 the Psalmist acknowledged possible partial shaking; by v6 the claim is absolute. The psalm's movement is from qualified trust (v2: not greatly shaken) to unqualified confidence (v6: not shaken at all). This growth of certainty within the psalm itself models how the act of preaching to one's own soul (v5: "Be still, my soul!") produces stronger faith.
7: עַל-אֱלֹהִים יִשְׁעִי וּכְבוֹדִי (ʿal-ʾĕlōhîm yishʿî ûkĕbôdî, "In God rests my salvation and my honor") — yeshaʿ: salvation/deliverance; kābôd: honor, weight, glory. Both rest (ʿal: upon) God. צוּר-עֻזִּי מַחְסִי בֵּאלֹהִים (ṣûr-ʿuzzî maḥsî bêʾlōhîm, "God is the rock of my strength, my refuge") — ṣûr ʿuzzî: "rock of my strength" — the rock metaphor extended: not just any rock but the source of strength. Maḥseh: the shelter/refuge (as in Psalms 46:1). The accumulation of images in this verse: God = salvation, honor, strength-rock, refuge.
8: בִּטְחוּ בוֹ בְכָל-עֵת עָם (bitḥû bô bĕkol-ʿêt ʿām, "Trust in him at all times, O people!") — the movement from individual to communal: the Psalmist who preached to his own soul (v5) now preaches the same trust to the whole people (ʿām). Bāṭaḥ (imperative: trust!). Bĕkol-ʿêt: at all times — not only in moments of crisis but continuously. שִׁפְכוּ-לְפָנָיו לְבַבְכֶם (shiphkû-lĕpānāyw lĕbabĕkem, "pour out your hearts before him") — shāpak: to pour out (as in Psalms 42:4 — the soul poured like water; here the heart poured before God in transparent prayer). אֱלֹהִים מַחֲסֶה-לָנוּ סֶלָה (ʾĕlōhîm maḥăseh-lānû selāh, "God is a refuge for us") — the confession extended to "us" — from the individual's "my refuge" (v7) to the community's "our refuge."
9: אַךְ הֶבֶל בְּנֵי-אָדָם (ʾak hebel bĕnê-ʾādām, "Surely common people are only a breath") — bĕnê-ʾādām: sons of Adam/common people (those of ordinary social standing). Hebel: breath, vapor, emptiness (the key word of Ecclesiastes — "vanity of vanities"). כָּזָב בְּנֵי-אִישׁ (kāzāb bĕnê-ʾîsh, "people of rank are a deception") — bĕnê-ʾîsh: "sons of man" (the men of distinction — ʾîsh as the person of standing). The low-status and the high-status are both weighed and found empty. בְּמֹאזְנַיִם לַעֲלוֹת (bĕmōʾznayim laʿălôt, "weighed in the scales they rise") — the scales of divine justice: placed in the balance, both categories of humanity literally rise (because they are lighter than hebel). The image of the balance going up when a weight is too light — the two classes of humanity together lighter than vapor.
10: אַל-תִּבְטְחוּ בְעֹשֶׁק (ʾal-tibṭĕḥû bĕʿōsheq, "Do not trust in extortion") — ʿōsheq: extortion, oppression, gain through wrongful squeezing of others. וּבְגָזֵל אַל-תֶּהְבָּלוּ (ûbĕgāzêl ʾal-tehbālû, "do not take false pride in robbery") — gāzal: robbery, seizure (forcible taking); hābal: to become empty, to be vain. חַיִל כִּי-יָנוּב אַל-תָּשִׂיתוּ לֵב (ḥayil kî-yānûb ʾal-tāśîtû lêb, "if wealth increases, do not set your heart on it") — ḥayil: wealth, strength (riches as power); nûb: to sprout, to increase (the organic growth of wealth). The warning: the growing of wealth creates a corresponding temptation to trust it. "Do not set your heart on it" — lêb (heart): the center of devotion and loyalty.
11: אַחַת דִּבֶּר אֱלֹהִים שְׁתַּיִם-זוּ שָׁמַעְתִּי (ʾaḥat dibbêr ʾĕlōhîm shĕttayim-zû shāmaʿtî, "One thing God has spoken; two things I have heard") — the numerical-saying structure (n/n+1): the same wisdom-speech pattern of Proverbs 6:16 ("There are six things the LORD hates, seven..."); Amos 1:3 ("For three transgressions... and for four"); Job 33:14. God speaks once; the attentive listener hears the fullness — "two things." The one divine speech contains more than one truth for the receptive ear. כִּי עֹז לֵאלֹהִים (kî ʿōz lêʾlōhîm, "that power belongs to God") — the first truth: ʿōz (strength, power) as God's exclusive possession (as in Psalms 29:1; Psalms 46:1). Power is not distributed; it belongs to God alone.
12: וּלְךָ-אֲדֹנָי חָסֶד (ûlĕkā-ʾădōnāy ḥāsed, "And to you, O Lord, belongs steadfast love") — the second truth heard from the one divine speech: ḥesed as the companion of ʿōz. Power (ʿōz) and covenant-loyalty (ḥesed) — the divine character is not power alone (which could be terrifying) but power in the service of covenant love. כִּי-אַתָּה תְשַׁלֵּם לְאִישׁ כְּמַעֲשֵׂהוּ (kî-ʾattāh tĕshallêm lĕʾîsh kĕmaʿăśêhû, "for you repay each person according to their deeds") — shālam (piel: to repay, to pay in full — the wholeness-root used for moral accountability). The divine repayment is the exercise of both attributes together: power ensures the repayment can be made; ḥesed ensures it is executed in faithfulness rather than arbitrary caprice. Paul cites this verse's principle in Romans 2:6.
